sentences.text: No, nobody was excited.
They were so happy to-- that they were right about it.
They just asked me what-- what-- what I'm gonna do.
And I said, well, maybe I'll just stay with you, because I told them where I was working, and who I was working for.
And everybody was listening.
And then they said, look, the war isn't over.
You are safe where you are, so don't throw it away yet.
Go back and continue being where you are.
We don't know what's going to happen with everybody.
We have nothing to lose.
But you have-- you have a safe place to stay, so stay for a while.
And I stayed for another few weeks, which wasn't easy anymore.
I was torn between looking-- once I knew that they were some Jews, I figured maybe there is somebody of my family who is left, even if it's-- if it's not the immediate family.
Maybe somebody is.
So I kept thinking about it, and dreaming about it.
And life became a little more complicated now.
So one day, I came to them, and there were two boys there in Russian uniforms.
It turned out that they were Jewish boys who-- who were in the partisans, but came out and worked for-- for the Russians, got Russian uniforms, and they worked for the Russian secret police.
And when I was introduced to them, they said, it's funny.
We know there are some people from your hometown in a different place.
They survived, and they settled in-- in Kiwerce, which was about an hour or two by train from where I was now.
And I-- I asked them what their names are, and if they know who it was.
They said no, but we can take you there.
And I said, well, I have no money to pay for the train, or a way of going there.
They said, we'll take you with us, and they did.
And they brought me to that town where I met some people from my hometown.
Two of them were parents of children who went to school with me, but they were single survivors, two men, their family have perished.
And they told me that my father was killed in the second pogram, that they don't know anybody of the whole family, not even a cousin, or a second cousin, or a third cousin who would survive.
They knew very few people who survived.
But I'm welcome to stay with them.
They all lived in one room, right on-- on the train station, which was a dangerous place to be.
That's why this house was empty, because they were bombarding the-- the trains.
So they had this house.
And since they told me I can stay with them, I did.
And life became a big problem.
Because there was no place to go to work, nobody had money, and there were no means of survival.
Nobody knew what to do.
So some of the men got jobs.
But for me, it was a problem.
I couldn't go back to school.
I had no roof over my head.
I had nobody I belonged to.
And-- and I didn't know where my next meal was coming from.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lady who-- who survived with her husband, and she was quite young, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had a baby.
And when she was hiding, there were more Jews hiding with her.
And when the baby started crying, they said to her that you have to make that baby stop crying because we're all gonna perish if they hear the baby cry.
So she had to put a pillow in her baby's face and-- and kill her own baby for the others to survive.
So she was emotional, very emotional about everything.
She said to me, I think I can make arrangements for all those men who are here.
If you cook for them, wash their clothes for them, you can eat, and you'll have something to eat.
So she made arrangements with them that I'll be eating with them for-- for the-- for doing those chores for them.
And those two boys who brought me there said that if I want to go back to school, they can take me with them, and I can stay with them and go to school.
So it sounded very good to me.
But she came up and she says, look, you are being very foolish.
Because one girl with two boys to live in the same house is not a very good thing to do.
And I think you're better off to stay with us.
So I stayed with them.
This is where I met my husband, because he was one of the men that stayed in that house.
And after maybe--
No.
Well, I stayed-- I stayed there for a while, and I got a bit nervous because I used to go to confession, to-- I would go with them to church, and she would say to me, you haven't been to confession.
You have to go to confession.
So I-- I was terrified, because I didn't really know what I'm supposed to confess to, or how I'm supposed to confess to.
I knew the prayers.
I knew how to cross myself, but I didn't know.
So I was always sitting close to the confession booth, and observing people, and listening in.
And then when I went to confession, I thought that I-- I knew exactly what to do.
And-- and I crossed myself, and I said, that-- I-- Father, I wanted to confess.
And I thought I did everything just perfect.
Then when you come after the confession, you kneel in front of-- of-- of the priest.
It's like a-- like a stage, and you kneel in front of that stage, which were about a dozen people or more.
You close your eyes, you open your mouth, and you get--
A wafer, so I did that.
But the priest went right by me and didn't give me the wafer.
And my heart almost stopped.
I didn't know if he just missed me by accident or he knew that I was Jewish and he didn't give it to me there and he wasn't fooled by anything that I said.
And I was scared.
I was waiting for him to go and tell the people that I worked for what my problem is.
And that didn't happen for a day, and it didn't happen for a while.
And I kept thinking that it's going to happen.
So I start asking for other people, if they knew who needed-- who need another-- a-- somebody to work for them.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And I found a different place, a smaller family.
It was closer to-- to town.
It was a few-- a few miles away from where I worked.
[SIGHS] I don't know how I figured I was safe there, but it was safer than going back to church.
I just didn't know how to handle the priest anymore.
So I found a place with a young family with two children.
Her husband was at war.
She was alone.
And I was doing the job that her husband was supposed to do in fields.
It was hard work.
But somehow, I managed.
I didn't really mind.
But she was extremely good to me.
She was very kind, she was like a friend to me, and I loved her children.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think of all the places I've been, I felt best there.
How long were you with her?
I have no idea of time or dates.
It didn't really matter.
Kaza was her first name, but I can't remember her second name.
And because she was young, she was sort of more like a friend to me.
She used to dress me up for church in her clothes, and tie a string around me, and pull it up if it was too long, and help me comb my hair.
And actually, I-- the reason I went away without saying goodbye to her is because I couldn't face her and tell her that I lied to her for so long.
Lying was a terrible thing.
It's not a easy thing to lie to people.
You're conscious of the fact that you're lying, and then you're afraid that you forget what you were saying to them.
And then you terrified if they ask you a question you hadn't prepared an answer for.
It was-- I maybe compare my life to other people during the war.
I had the good life.
I wasn't hungry.
And-- but a strenuous, very strenuous.
I could never confess to anybody.
I could never open up to anybody.
I could never ask anybody for advice.
I was afraid to be sick, because in case I have fever, and I-- and I say things I'm not supposed to.
I was always aware that I might do something that isn't right, especially working on a farm which I wasn't used to.
Every time I've done something wrong, I was terrified if they ask me, how come you didn't know how to do it the right way?
I constantly observe people, and I was constantly aware of pleasing everybody around me and not getting into any problems with anybody.
Not to talk too much, or say too much, and remembering what I said when I said it so I would repeat the same thing.
I was liberated in that place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I met my husband.
No, I went to the Jewish people, and there I met him.
No.
I was working-- Kaza took me to work in that city I was working, then I went to the Jewish people.
And there I met the two partisans, they took me to-- to Kiwerce.
And--
No, I was liberated when I was at Kaza's.
Well, to me it was no liberation.
I mean, the Russians came and--
No, didn't change my life.
No, this was in Kiwerce already, where I met my husband.
And when my husband-- I met my husband after a while.
He proposed, and we got married.
And--
Yeah, we stayed for a while in that town.
Then when our city was liberated, Wlodzimierz, we went there.
No, he was from Zamosc, but he went through the-- he went through the occupation in my city, and he lost his family there.
Nothing very much.
We continued because--
No.
I-- we-- we-- can't remember where we stayed.
We stayed for a while with some friends, I think.
Oh, yeah I knew by then that nobody is alive.
Just found-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I go over there.
I think it was standing.
I-- I don't know.
I really don't know if I even went.
I think we stayed with friends, somebody who-- who survived.
We didn't stay too long.
We continued.
We went to my husband's town.
And from there, we went to-- we were-- we were scared, sort of, to-- to-- then-- then we went to my husband's town.
From there, we went to another small town.
But that time, I was pregnant already.
Not very long.
We stayed in [INAUDIBLE], which was not far from his town.
We stayed there for a while, and that's when I had my first baby, which died at childbirth.
And we buried her there, and then we continued.
No, at home.
It was a very tragic thing.
And then we went-- we continued searching for-- we went to [?
Sterchin, ?] and from [?
Sterchin, ?] we went to Germany.
And--
A few months, I think.
He was always in-- in business one way or another.
He'd open a little shop, or a stand, or somewhere just to make-- long enough to make a living.
And then we-- we went to-- to Germany.
We stayed in Berlin, and--
A transit camp.
Yeah, Schlachtensee.
I think a few months, then we moved to Stuttgart.
And there we lived for about a year.
He-- he was in business, he had an antique shop.
I-- There was very little place for women to work in Europe.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- actually, all we wanted to do is get out of there.
We wanted to immigrate, but it wasn't easy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we moved to Munich, and this is where my oldest daughter was born.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was-- And she was eight months old.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] After my first baby, I had a very hard time.
I wanted very much to have a child.
But after the first baby was born, I don't know what was wrong.
I just couldn't become pregnant, and I thought I never will.
It took me a long time with a lot of going to doctors that I became pregnant with my oldest daughter.
And when she was born, I thought it was just a miracle that happened to me once and it never will.
But to my surprise, I became pregnant again.
And I kept telling my husband that no way is my child going to be born in Germany.
I do not want that to happen.
My first one was born because I had no choice, but this time I want to go.
And because most of our friends had immigrated already, some to Canada, some to the United States, they sent us papers.
We received papers to go to New York, and we received papers to go to Canada.
Yeah.
And we couldn't make up our mind.
No.
One was a friend, and one was a-- a far relative.
So we didn't know where we gonna choose to go.
It didn't matter to us.
We just wanted to get out.
So my husband says, what are we gonna do?
Because we received papers to come and see the consul.
I said, well, whichever's gonna call us first, that's where we going to go.
And it happened that the Canadian consul called us for Thursday, and the American for Friday.
So since I had said that already, he stuck to that idea, and we went to the Canadian consul.
And because I was pregnant with my second child, they made sure that I got out before seven months pregnancy.
And we came by boat to Halifax.
It was called It was sort of like a military boat.
And from Halifax, we traveled by train to Winnipeg.
We came in 1950, in May.
Winnipeg had a big flood that year, and we arrived in a very-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very bad time.
We came to-- to a second cousin of mine.
We stayed for a few days with them, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe even a couple of weeks, then we started looking for a place to rent.
We rented a house with a Mr.
Roytenberg who had-- who was on [BACKROUND NOISE] [?
Mynutoba ?] Avenue.
His wife had died.
He was alone, so he shared the house with us.
And--
He was looking for work, he couldn't get work, but he opened a-- a store with-- with a partner.
The partner was a Canadian, a Mr.
Cohen. ?] And they bought a store on Gordon Avenue that was open from 8:00 in the morning to 12:00 midnight.
It was the only store in Winnipeg that was open that late, and he start going from the north end to the south end by bus every day, and staying till midnight.
And by the time he came home, it was quite late and getting up in the morning again.
But it was good times.
After we arrived, my second daughter was born in Grace Hospital on September the 8th, 1950.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then a few years later, I had my son and then my baby daughter.
All in Winnipeg except the one in Germany.
The oldest one.
